title: Why We're Building the “Rig Carlton”
excerpt: Founder Jeff Swenson on building resort-grade truck terminals — secure parking, shore power, and the Outriders Drivers Club — that bring back driver pride.
hero_alt: Semi truck lit by golden sunlight on the open highway
seo_title: Why We're Building the “Rig Carlton” | LineHaul Station
body:
I grew up in the early 1970s revering truck drivers. To a young boy, they were icons — men and women who walked tall and proud, hardworking ambassadors of commerce and, the way I saw it, knights of the highway. They were out there to help and protect Americans in need while traveling miles of brand-new freeway. You could tell a driver by the way they carried themselves: neatly dressed, polished boots, confident, full of life. And the rigs — spotless inside and out, chrome shined like a mirror. There was pride in all of it.

Somewhere along the way, the industry stopped honoring that. The job got harder, and the places drivers were expected to rest got worse. The typical terminal became a dingy, poorly lit lot you tolerated, not a place you were proud to pull into. We think that's backwards. So we set out to build the opposite.

## A truck terminal that feels like a resort

LineHaul Station terminals are developed on 50-plus-acre sites at the edge of major metro markets, along the highest-density freight lanes. When you roll through the access-controlled gate, the arrival experience is meant to restore your sense of pride — beautiful landscaping, real architecture, proper lighting. It feels more like driving into a resort than a truck terminal. Some drivers have already nicknamed it the “Rig Carlton.” I'll be honest: I kind of love that.

### Built for the way trucks actually move

Inside the gates, the property is built for real-world driving — extra-wide drive lanes and parking spaces that cut down on the minor accidents that turn into big headaches and delays. Every space has high-speed Wi-Fi and a 50-amp shore power connection, so you can shut the engine off, skip the idling, and sleep in a quiet environment.

## Inside the Outriders Drivers Club

Then there's the Outriders Drivers Club. It's a rustic, hip space loaded with the kind of amenities most drivers never get on the road: a lodge-style bistro, a well-equipped fitness center, clean locker and shower rooms, a vintage barbershop, a gamer studio, our own LineHaul gear shop, and a sky deck with fire pits and cornhole. Your truck gets treated too, with a full-service inspection, maintenance and repair center and a state-of-the-art truck wash.

None of this is luxury for luxury's sake. It's what respect looks like in physical form. You deserve to be treated with dignity for the hard work you do, and we intend to make sure you get it.

## This is bigger than a nicer building

The “Rig Carlton” nickname is fun, but the mission underneath it is serious. We're building the country's first national, shared-use truck terminal network — and we're doing it as a private, member-only network for the truckload freight sector. That model lets us deliver resort-grade facilities at a price that works, because the cost is shared instead of carried alone. (New to how that works? Start with [Shared Terminals 101](/blog/shared-terminals-101).)

We're bringing back the pride that defined trucking's best era. If that resonates with you, come be part of it.

title: The Hidden Cost of Nowhere to Park
excerpt: Truck parking shortages cost drivers their safety and carriers real money. Here's the true price of nowhere to park — and how a shared-use terminal network fixes it.
hero_alt: Semi trucks parked at a rest area at night
seo_title: The Hidden Cost of Nowhere to Park | LineHaul Station
body:
Every working driver knows the feeling. The clock on your hours of service is running down, you're still miles from a safe spot, and the lots are already full. So you stop early and burn productive time, or you push your luck and end up parked where you shouldn't — an off-ramp, a side street, a dirty lot, somewhere that isn't legal and isn't safe. It's one of the most stressful, least talked-about parts of the job. And it's costing the industry far more than anyone admits.

## A safety problem first

When a driver can't find legal parking, safety is the first casualty. Off-ramp and shoulder parking puts drivers — and the motoring public — at real risk. Side streets and unlit lots invite theft and worse. None of this is a failure of the driver. It's a failure of infrastructure. There simply aren't enough quality places to stop, and the ones that exist weren't built with the driver's security, rest, or dignity in mind.

We feel strongly that it's high time this changed. Every driver deserves access to a high-quality terminal network — regardless of the size of the fleet they drive for — that provides the security, amenities, and peace of mind they want, in the locations they actually need.

## A productivity problem too

The parking shortage doesn't just wear drivers down; it quietly drags on the whole operation. Stopping early to secure a spot means leaving drive time on the table. Hunting for parking burns fuel and patience. And the ripple effects show up on the carrier's and broker's books — in detention, in out-of-route miles, in trucks and drivers that aren't where they need to be.

This is why we keep saying the win is shared. When drivers have a reliable, secure home base along the freight lanes they run, the companies they serve win big too — with real gains in efficiency and productivity.

### What carriers and brokers gain

- Reduce shipper detention
- Increase drop-and-hook
- Decrease out-of-route miles
- Enable freight relay so more drivers get home daily

## What a real solution looks like

LineHaul Station is building the country's first national, shared-use truck terminal network to solve exactly this. Our hubs sit on 50-plus-acre sites at the edge of major metros, along the highest-density freight lanes — right where parking pressure is worst. Each space comes with secure, access-controlled entry, extra-wide lanes, high-speed Wi-Fi, and 50-amp shore power so you can rest without idling.

With this new shared infrastructure, it provides solutions where everyone in trucking wins. (Curious how membership and Space ownership work? Read [Shared Terminals 101](/blog/shared-terminals-101).)

## Nowhere to park shouldn't be the cost of doing the job

The current system asks drivers to absorb a problem they didn't create. We think that's unacceptable, and we're building the network to fix it.

title: Shared Terminals 101: How Membership Works
excerpt: How LineHaul Station's shared-use terminal network works — free driver membership, Space and FlexSpace ownership, digital passes, and the refundable “one in, one out” program.
hero_alt: Highway convoy of semi trucks at dusk
seo_title: Shared Terminals 101: How Membership Works | LineHaul Station
body:
For decades, a national terminal network was something only the largest carriers could afford to build. The cost of land, construction, and operations put it out of reach for the tens of thousands of small and mid-size carriers and brokers who move the majority of America's freight — and, by extension, out of reach for the more than two million drivers they employ. LineHaul Station changes that by sharing the infrastructure. Here's how the model works in plain terms.

## A private, member-only network

LineHaul Station is a private, member-only terminal network built for the truckload freight sector. “Member-only” matters: it keeps the network accountable, safe, and built around people who act responsibly and have each other's back — the way the best of trucking always worked. There are two levels of membership.

## Level 1: Driver membership (currently free)

Driver membership is currently free and takes about two minutes to complete online. Its purpose is simple — to make sure every member acts responsibly and looks out for the rest. There's no obligation; if you never use it, you've lost nothing. You just complete your membership agreement online. As Jeff puts it, you have nothing to lose and everything to gain.

## Level 2: Space membership

The second level is called **Space**. This is for the company or driver that buys and owns the rights to a space in the network. Think of it like owning a truck terminal of your own — except you don't have to buy the whole terminal. We sell it one space at a time, and in some cases even a fraction of a space.

### Dedicated Space vs. FlexSpace

A Space can be **dedicated** to a single hub, or it can be **FlexSpace** that's usable across the network as it grows. Most carriers and freight brokers purchase Space and then distribute **digital passes** to their drivers for daily use. It's an easy decision, because the cost is far less than independently buying or building a terminal — and members are blown away by the features and amenities, with dues as low as $13 a day.

## The “one member in, one member out” program

Here's the part that makes Space ownership feel different from a typical lease or fee. LineHaul Station runs a **one member in, one member out** program, and the majority of your initial membership fee is refundable when the membership is retired — for whatever reason. Think of it like buying and later selling any other property you own. You're not pouring money into a hole; you're holding an asset.

## Why sharing wins for everyone

Most carriers genuinely try to give drivers the tools of the trade, but some things just aren't economically feasible for a single company to build alone. By sharing space, LineHaul Station creates an enormous win-win for every stakeholder:

- Drivers get secure parking, shore power, showers, food, and a real home base on their lanes.
- Carriers and brokers can affordably build or expand their terminal footprint, reduce shipper detention, increase drop-and-hook, decrease out-of-route miles, and enable freight relay so more drivers get home daily.
- The industry finally gets the national terminal network it has needed for decades — open to fleets of any size.

## Getting started

If you're a driver, complete your free membership agreement to join the Outriders. If you're a carrier or broker, the fastest path is a conversation about how much Space fits your lanes and how digital passes would work for your drivers.

title: Shore Power vs. Idling: The Real Math
excerpt: What a 50-amp shore power connection actually saves a driver every night — in fuel, engine wear, and a quiet cab — versus idling the truck just to stay comfortable.
hero_alt: A truck plugged into a shore power pedestal at a LineHaul Station terminal
seo_title: Shore Power vs. Idling: The Real Math | LineHaul Station
body:
A truck idling overnight to keep the cab livable burns roughly a gallon of diesel an hour. Over a ten-hour rest, that's real money — and it's money spent to sit still. Multiply it across a career and idling quietly becomes one of the largest line items a driver never sees on a single receipt.

## What idling actually costs

Beyond the fuel, idling runs the engine for hours it isn't moving freight. That means added wear, more frequent maintenance intervals, and shortened component life — all to do something the truck was never designed to do: act as a generator. And it happens in a cab that's never quite quiet, parked next to a row of other trucks doing the same thing.

## What shore power changes

Every parking space at a LineHaul Station hub includes a 50-amp shore power connection. You plug in, shut the engine off, and run your climate control, electronics, and appliances off the pedestal instead of the diesel engine. The cab goes quiet. The fuel stops burning. And you rest in an environment built for sleep, not one you're tolerating.

### The nightly math, simply

- No idle fuel burned during your rest
- Fewer engine hours that don't move freight, and less wear
- A quiet, climate-controlled cab for genuine sleep
- Lower emissions at the terminal for everyone parked there

## Small per night, large per year

The savings on any single night look modest. The point is that you stop every night, and the network is built so that stopping no longer costs you fuel, engine wear, and sleep all at once. (For the bigger picture on how the terminals are built, read [Why We're Building the “Rig Carlton”](/blog/why-were-building-the-rig-carlton).)

Cleaner, quieter, cheaper rest isn't a luxury feature. It's what a parking space should have included all along.

title: Drop-and-Hook: The Operational Win Hiding in Plain Sight
excerpt: Drop-and-hook turns a driver's wait into someone else's drive time. Here's how a shared terminal network finally makes it practical — and what it does to detention and utilization.
hero_alt: Loaded trailers staged in a secure drop yard at dusk
seo_title: Drop-and-Hook: The Operational Win Hiding in Plain Sight | LineHaul Station
body:
Ask any dispatcher where the hours go and you'll hear the same answer: waiting. Waiting to load, waiting to unload, waiting on a door. Live loads turn a driver's clock into someone else's schedule. Drop-and-hook flips that — the driver drops a trailer, hooks a pre-loaded one, and is back on the road in minutes instead of hours.

## Why it's hard to do today

Drop-and-hook only works if there's a secure place to stage trailers near the lane and the metro you serve. Without that, you're back to live loads, tight appointment windows, and detention. The missing piece has never been the willingness — it's the real estate and the security to park trailers safely between legs.

## What a shared network unlocks

A national network of secure, access-controlled hubs at the edge of major metros gives carriers and brokers exactly that staging ground — without each company having to buy and build its own yard in every market. Trailers sit behind a controlled gate on the lane you already run, ready to hook.

### What it does to the numbers

- Less driver time lost to live loading and detention
- Higher tractor and driver utilization
- More predictable appointment windows for shippers
- Trailers staged on-lane instead of deadheading to find a yard

## The win is shared

When a driver spends minutes instead of hours at the terminal, the carrier gets more productive miles, the broker gets more reliable coverage, and the shipper gets freight that moves on schedule. (New to how owning Space in the network works? Start with [Shared Terminals 101](/blog/shared-terminals-101).)

Drop-and-hook isn't a new idea. A network that finally makes it practical for fleets of any size is.

title: Freight Relay: How More Drivers Get Home Every Night
excerpt: A national terminal network makes relay practical — handing freight off mid-lane so two regional drivers sleep at home instead of one team living in the truck coast to coast.
hero_alt: Two drivers swapping trailers at a relay point on the freight lane
seo_title: Freight Relay: How More Drivers Get Home Every Night | LineHaul Station
body:
The longest hauls are the ones that keep drivers away from home the longest. A single team running coast to coast can move a load fast, but it does it by living in the truck for days at a time. Relay offers another way: hand the freight off mid-lane so two regional drivers each run a leg and sleep in their own beds.

## Why relay needs a network

Relay only works if there's a reliable, secure place to make the handoff — a known point on the lane where one driver drops and another picks up, any hour, safely. Without that fixed infrastructure, relay is a logistics headache. With it, relay becomes routine.

## Regional legs, national reach

LineHaul Station hubs sit at the edge of major metros along the highest-density freight lanes — natural handoff points. A load can move across the country in regional legs, each driver turning back toward home at the hub instead of pressing on for another day away.

### Who gains

- Drivers get home time without giving up miles
- Carriers get a deeper, more stable regional driver pool
- Freight keeps moving around the clock through scheduled handoffs

## Home time is retention

The hardest part of keeping good drivers is the time the job takes them away from home. A network that makes relay practical turns long-haul freight into a series of regional runs — and turns more nights on the road into more nights at home. (For the safety and parking side of the same network, see [The Hidden Cost of Nowhere to Park](/blog/hidden-cost-of-nowhere-to-park).)

Move the freight the same distance. Just let more drivers go home along the way.

title: A Day at the Outriders Club
excerpt: Shower, hot meal, gym, barber, a quiet bunk and a sky deck — a walk through what a stop at the Outriders Drivers Club actually feels like after a long haul.
hero_alt: Lodge-style driver lounge inside the Outriders Club
seo_title: A Day at the Outriders Club | LineHaul Station
body:
Pull through the access-controlled gate, park in an extra-wide space, plug into shore power, and shut the engine off. From here, the rest of the stop looks nothing like the lot you're used to.

## First, the basics done right

A clean, private locker and shower room. A lodge-style bistro with a real hot meal instead of whatever's left under a heat lamp. Fast Wi-Fi that actually reaches your truck. The things every driver should be able to count on, treated like they matter.

## Then, the parts that feel like a club

There's a well-equipped fitness center for working off a long day behind the wheel, a vintage barbershop, a gamer studio, and the LineHaul gear shop. Up top, a sky deck with fire pits and cornhole — somewhere to actually be a person for an hour before the next leg.

### While you rest, your truck gets looked after

- Full-service inspection, maintenance, and repair center
- State-of-the-art truck wash
- Secure, access-controlled parking the whole time you're there

## Not luxury for its own sake

None of this is luxury for luxury's sake. It's what respect looks like in physical form — the difference between a place you tolerate and a place you're glad to pull into. (For the why behind it, read [Why We're Building the “Rig Carlton”](/blog/why-were-building-the-rig-carlton).)

You do hard work. A stop should leave you better than it found you. That's the whole idea.

title: Member-Only by Design: How We Keep the Network Safe
excerpt: Access-controlled gates, digital passes, and a member-only model aren't red tape — they're how a terminal stays a place where drivers and cargo are actually safe.
hero_alt: Access-controlled entry gate at a LineHaul Station terminal at night
seo_title: Member-Only by Design: How We Keep the Network Safe | LineHaul Station
body:
People sometimes ask why a truck terminal would be member-only. Isn't more open always better? In trucking, the answer is no — and the reason is the same one the best terminals always ran on: everyone there has a stake in looking out for the place and for each other.

## Security starts at the gate

Every LineHaul Station hub is access-controlled. Drivers enter with digital passes their carrier or broker issues; trailers sit behind a controlled gate. That single fact changes the math for anyone thinking about cargo theft — the lot isn't anonymous, and it isn't open to whoever happens to pull in.

## A member-only network is an accountable one

Member-only keeps the network built around people who act responsibly and have each other's back. Driver membership exists precisely to make sure every member agrees to that standard. It isn't red tape; it's the thing that keeps a terminal safe enough to be worth stopping at.

### What the model protects

- Cargo, behind access-controlled gates instead of in open lots
- Drivers, resting somewhere secure and well-lit
- The network's character, kept to members who act responsibly

## Safe enough to actually rest

Security isn't only about cargo. It's whether a driver can close their eyes and sleep. A member-only, access-controlled network is how you get there. (For how membership and passes work, read [Shared Terminals 101](/blog/shared-terminals-101).)

Open to fleets of any size, closed to the chaos that makes the average lot a place you'd rather not stop — that's the balance member-only is designed to strike.

title: Built at the Edge of the Metro: Why Location Decides Everything
excerpt: Why LineHaul Station puts 50-plus-acre hubs at the edge of major metros, on the densest freight lanes — and what that placement does for drivers, carriers, and shippers.
hero_alt: Aerial view of a large terminal site at the edge of a metro area
seo_title: Built at the Edge of the Metro: Why Location Decides Everything | LineHaul Station
body:
A terminal is only as useful as its location. Put it in the wrong place and the best amenities in the world won't save the drive time, the deadhead, and the missed appointment windows. So before LineHaul Station builds anything, it answers one question first: where does this load actually need to stop?

## At the edge, on the lane

LineHaul Station develops 50-plus-acre sites at the edge of major metro markets, along the highest-density freight lanes. That placement is deliberate. It's close enough to the metro to serve its freight, but positioned where land allows a real terminal — extra-wide lanes, hundreds of spaces, and room for the Outriders Club and the Service Center.

## Why the edge beats the center

Downtown is where freight is going, not where trucks can stop. The edge of the metro is where the lane and the market meet — the natural place to stage trailers, make relay handoffs, and rest before the final delivery leg. Get the location right and drop-and-hook, relay, and detention all improve at once.

### What the right site makes possible

- Secure staging within reach of the metro's freight
- Natural relay handoff points along the lane
- Room for resort-grade amenities a cramped urban lot can't fit
- Shorter, more predictable final delivery legs

## Location is the strategy

The network's value compounds because each hub is placed where freight already moves. (For what that unlocks operationally, read [Drop-and-Hook: The Operational Win Hiding in Plain Sight](/blog/drop-and-hook-operational-win).)

Build at the edge of the metro, on the densest lanes, and the terminal stops being a detour. It becomes part of the route.
